regions.name: Thuringia
regions.bestTimeToVisit: April to October for pleasant weather and forest scenery.

top10.name: Berlin
top10.description: Though technically in Berlin, the city dominates Brandenburg's geography and culture, offering world-class museums, historical sites like the Brandenburg Gate and Reichstag, and vibrant neighborhoods. No visit to Brandenburg is complete without exploring Germany's capital.

top10.name: Munich
top10.description: Bavaria's vibrant capital, known for its beer halls, palaces, and the famous Oktoberfest celebration. Home to world-class museums, Marienplatz's Gothic town hall, and the stunning Neuschwanstein Castle nearby, making it the essential Bavarian experience.

top10.name: Cologne
top10.description: Germany's fourth-largest city is dominated by the stunning UNESCO-listed Cologne Cathedral, a Gothic masterpiece that took 600 years to complete and towers over the Rhine. The city blends medieval charm with vibrant museums, excellent restaurants, and riverside atmosphere, making it the region's essential cultural hub.

top10.name: Hamburg
top10.description: Germany's second-largest city and a major port on the Elbe River, Hamburg captivates visitors with its picturesque canals, historic warehouses, and bustling harbor. The city's blend of maritime heritage, modern architecture, and cultural attractions make it a top destination.

top10.name: Frankfurt
top10.description: Home to Germany's largest airport, Frankfurt is Europe's financial center and gateway for international visitors exploring the country. Tourists visit for its impressive skyline, world-class museums, historic Römerberg district, and excellent transportation links.

top10.name: Neuschwanstein Castle
top10.description: The fairy-tale castle perched high above the village of Hohenschwangau that inspired Disney's Sleeping Beauty Castle. One of Germany's most photographed monuments and an absolute must-see for its romantic architecture and Alpine setting.

top10.name: Heidelberg
top10.description: Germany's most romantic city features a stunning castle ruin perched above the Neckar River and a charming Altstadt with a famous university atmosphere. The combination of historic architecture, riverside setting, and nearby forest hills makes it one of the country's most visited destinations.

top10.name: Dresden
top10.description: Saxony's stunning capital, rebuilt after World War II destruction, showcases Baroque architecture and world-class museums along the Elbe River. The Frauenkirche, Semper Opera House, and Dresden Castle are essential stops for anyone visiting eastern Germany.

top10.name: Füssen and the Bavarian Alps
top10.description: This picturesque town serves as the main gateway to Neuschwanstein Castle and the stunning Bavarian Alpine region, offering hiking, scenic landscapes, and traditional Bavarian culture. Visitors come for the castle visits, mountain scenery, and outdoor recreation opportunities.

top10.name: Black Forest
top10.description: Germany's largest mountain range, renowned for its dense evergreen forests, charming villages, and traditional culture including cuckoo clocks and Black Forest cake. The region offers spectacular hiking trails, scenic drives, and a quintessential German countryside experience with picturesque farmhouses.

places-index.json.name: Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe
places-index.json.nameIt: Denkmal für die ermordeten Juden Europas

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 43 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: Neujahrskonzert der Berliner Philharmoniker
festivals.description: The Berlin Philharmonic's prestigious New Year's concert, broadcast worldwide and featuring classical masterworks. A cornerstone of German cultural tradition since 1882.

festivals.name: Neujahrstag
festivals.description: New Year's Day, a nationwide public holiday celebrating the start of the new year. Banks, shops and most businesses are closed.

festivals.name: International Filmfestspiele Berlin
festivals.description: One of the world's most prestigious film festivals showcasing international cinema and hosting major red-carpet premieres. A highlight of the global film calendar.

festivals.name: Fasching
festivals.description: Bavaria's spectacular carnival season with elaborate parades, costumes and street celebrations. Known for its distinctly Bavarian twist on traditional carnival festivities.

festivals.name: Cologne Carnival
festivals.description: The Rhineland's most exuberant carnival with three days of street parades, music and traditional costumes. Famous for its satirical floats and 'Drei Jecken' celebration.

festivals.name: Düsseldorf Carnival
festivals.description: A vibrant Rhineland carnival with elaborate processions and traditional Düsseldorf humor. Known for its unique blend of music, dance and satirical performances.

festivals.name: Mainz Carnival
festivals.description: The Rhineland's oldest carnival with traditional processions and political satire. A centuries-old celebration of Mainz's unique carnival culture.

festivals.name: Karfreitag
festivals.description: Good Friday, a nationwide public holiday observed two days before Easter Sunday. Most businesses close for this significant Christian holiday.

festivals.name: Ostersonntag
festivals.description: Easter Sunday, celebrated across Germany with family gatherings, Easter markets and egg hunts. A major nationwide religious holiday.

festivals.name: Ostermontag
festivals.description: Easter Monday, the day after Easter Sunday and a nationwide public holiday. Businesses and schools remain closed.

festivals.name: Wagner Festival
festivals.description: The world's oldest music festival dedicated exclusively to Richard Wagner's operas. Held in the specially designed Festspielhaus with performances of the complete Ring cycle.

festivals.name: Oktoberfest
festivals.description: The world's largest beer festival celebrating Bavarian culture with traditional beer halls, food and folk music. A global phenomenon attracting millions annually.

festivals.name: Tag der Deutschen Einheit
festivals.description: German Unity Day, celebrating the reunification of Germany on October 3, 1990. A nationwide public holiday marked by patriotic celebrations and ceremonies.

festivals.name: Leipzig Book Fair
festivals.description: Germany's oldest book fair and one of Europe's largest literary events showcasing publishers, authors and readers. A cornerstone of German literary culture.

festivals.name: Frankfurt Book Fair
festivals.description: The world's largest international book fair attracting publishers and authors from around the globe. A major event in the global publishing calendar.

festivals.name: Documenta
festivals.description: A legendary contemporary art exhibition held every five years, showcasing cutting-edge modern and contemporary art. One of the world's most influential art exhibitions.

festivals.name: Love Parade
festivals.description: A massive electronic dance music festival and techno parade celebrating music culture. An iconic Berlin event drawing hundreds of thousands of visitors.

festivals.name: Nuremberg Christkindlesmarkt
festivals.description: Germany's most famous Christmas market with over 180 wooden stalls selling crafts and traditional Nuremberg specialties. Dating back to the 16th century.

festivals.name: Cologne Christmas Markets
festivals.description: Multiple enchanting Christmas markets throughout Cologne featuring traditional crafts, mulled wine and festive atmosphere. The city's most magical season.

festivals.name: Berlin Christmas Markets
festivals.description: Numerous festive Christmas markets across Berlin offering holiday treats and traditional crafts. A quintessential Berlin winter experience.

festivals.name: Hamburg Port Anniversary
festivals.description: A three-day celebration of Hamburg's historic port with historical ship parades, cultural performances and maritime festivities. One of Germany's oldest harbor celebrations.

festivals.name: Kiel Week
festivals.description: The world's largest sailing festival featuring international yacht races and maritime celebrations. A premier event for sailing enthusiasts globally.

festivals.name: Wacken Open Air
festivals.description: Europe's largest open-air heavy metal festival attracting over 75,000 metalheads from around the world. A pilgrimage site for metal music fans.

festivals.name: Rock am Ring
festivals.description: Germany's largest rock and pop music festival with multiple stages and international headliners. A major annual gathering for rock and alternative music fans.

festivals.name: Carnival of Cultures
festivals.description: Berlin's multicultural street festival celebrating diverse cultures with parades, music and food. A vibrant display of Berlin's cosmopolitan character.

festivals.name: Deutschlandfest
festivals.description: A nationwide celebration of German Unity Day featuring outdoor festivals, cultural events and music across the country. Coincides with October 3rd festivities.

festivals.name: Heidelberg Theater Festival
festivals.description: A prestigious international theater festival in romantic Heidelberg showcasing classical and contemporary performances. Held in atmospheric historic venues.

festivals.name: Asparagus Festival
festivals.description: A culinary celebration of white asparagus harvest season with tastings, cooking demonstrations and regional dishes. A spring tradition in Germany's asparagus country.

festivals.name: Dresdner Musikfestspielen
festivals.description: Dresden's prestigious classical music festival featuring world-class orchestras and soloists in beautiful historic venues. A major event in European classical music calendar.

festivals.name: Weimar Classical Open
festivals.description: An international classical music festival celebrating Weimar's rich cultural heritage and connections to Goethe and Schiller. Performances held in historic venues.

festivals.name: Berlin Marathon
festivals.description: The world's largest marathon attracting over 40,000 runners with a route through Berlin's historic neighborhoods. A global sporting spectacle.

festivals.name: Pfingstsonntag
festivals.description: Whit Sunday, the seventh Sunday after Easter, is a nationwide public holiday. Marks the descent of the Holy Spirit in Christian tradition.

festivals.name: Pfingstmontag
festivals.description: Whit Monday, the day after Whit Sunday, is a nationwide public holiday. Businesses and schools remain closed.

festivals.name: Ascension Day
festivals.description: Ascension Day, celebrated 39 days after Easter, is a nationwide public holiday marking Jesus's ascension. A major Christian observance across Germany.

festivals.name: Tag der Arbeit
festivals.description: Labor Day, a nationwide public holiday celebrating workers and labor movements. International Workers' Day with various demonstrations and festivities.

festivals.name: Karneval der Kulturen
festivals.description: A multicultural celebration showcasing Berlin's diverse communities through parades, music and food. One of Berlin's most colorful festivals.

festivals.name: Jena Light Festival
festivals.description: An innovative light art festival transforming Jena's streets with installations and projections. A unique celebration of contemporary light and art.

festivals.name: Bregenzer Festspiele Overflow
festivals.description: Cultural events associated with Austria's Bregenz Festival activities near the German-Austrian border. A major regional musical and theatrical celebration.

festivals.name: Bremer Freimarkt
festivals.description: One of Germany's oldest fairs dating back to 1035 with traditional rides, food and carnival atmosphere. A centuries-old autumn celebration.

festivals.name: Hanover Industrial Fair
festivals.description: The world's largest industrial technology trade fair showcasing innovations and cutting-edge technology. A premier international business and technology event.

festivals.name: Allerheiligen
festivals.description: All Saints' Day is a public holiday in certain German regions, particularly Catholic areas. Traditionally observed with cemetery visits and commemorations.

festivals.name: Fronleichnam
festivals.description: Corpus Christi, celebrated 60 days after Easter, is a public holiday in Catholic regions. Marked by elaborate processions and religious ceremonies.

festivals.name: Silvester
festivals.description: New Year's Eve with fireworks, parties and celebrations across Germany. A nationwide festive night welcoming the new year.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Germany
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 15 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Hunter-Gatherers and Early Settlement
items.summary: Nomadic peoples gradually settled the Germanic lands after the Ice Age, developing agriculture and bronze-working.
items.text: As the glaciers retreated from northern Europe, hunter-gatherer bands followed migrating herds into the fertile river valleys of what would become Germany. Over millennia, these communities adopted farming techniques from the Mediterranean, domesticating cattle and cultivating grains in the rich loess soils. By around 3000 BC, the Bronze Age brought metalworking and more complex social hierarchies, with fortified settlements emerging along the Rhine and Danube. These early Germans developed the first stirrings of tribal identity, though they remained fragmented and without central authority.

items.label: Celtic and Germanic Iron Age
items.summary: Celtic tribes dominated the south while Germanic peoples consolidated in the north, developing distinct cultures and warfare traditions.
items.text: Celtic tribes such as the Helvetii and Boii controlled much of what is now southern Germany during the Iron Age, building hill forts and engaging in long-distance trade with Mediterranean civilizations. Meanwhile, Germanic tribes—including the Cherusci, Marcomanni, and Chatti—occupied the northern and central regions, developing their own distinct languages, gods, and warrior codes. These Germanic peoples were organized into loose confederations led by warrior aristocrats, with economies based on livestock herding, agriculture, and increasingly aggressive raiding. The region became a cultural and military frontier, with Celtic and Germanic identities sharply diverging by the time of Roman contact.

items.label: Roman Conquest and the Limes
items.summary: Rome conquered the west bank of the Rhine, dividing Germanic lands and creating a frontier of military camps and fortifications.
items.text: Julius Caesar's campaigns in Gaul (58–50 BC) brought Roman legions to the Rhine, establishing it as the empire's natural border with the Germanic lands beyond. The Romans built a massive defensive line called the Limes, studded with forts, watchtowers, and wooden palisades that stretched nearly 350 miles from the North Sea to the Danube. The regions west of the Rhine—including parts of modern Germany—were incorporated into the provinces of Germania Inferior and Germania Superior, bringing Roman law, cities, roads, and eventually Christianity to settled populations. Germanic tribes east of the Rhine remained independent and frequently raided Roman territory, though they also traded and adopted Roman goods and customs, creating a complex relationship of commerce and conflict that lasted centuries.

items.label: Frankish Ascendancy and Charlemagne
items.summary: Germanic Franks conquered Rome's western territories and Charlemagne built a vast empire that would shape European Christianity and politics.
items.text: After Rome's collapse, the Frankish kingdom emerged as the dominant Germanic power under the Merovingian and then Carolingian dynasties. Charlemagne (r. 768–814) expanded his realm into a continental empire, systematically conquering the Saxons in brutal campaigns and forcing Christianization across all Germanic lands. In 800, the Pope crowned Charlemagne emperor, reviving the concept of a unified Western empire and making him a foundational figure for both Germany and Europe. His court at Aachen became a center of learning and administration, spreading literacy and monastic culture throughout the German-speaking regions.

items.label: Partition and the Birth of Germany
items.summary: Charlemagne's empire fragmented, and the eastern portion evolved into the Kingdom of Germany, establishing the foundations of the German nation.
items.text: When Charlemagne's grandsons divided his empire at the Treaty of Verdun in 843, the eastern portion—roughly bounded by the Rhine, Danube, and North Sea—became the Kingdom of Germany under Louis the German. This realm gradually developed its own identity distinct from France and Italy, with German-speaking dukes and bishops governing semi-autonomous duchies like Bavaria, Saxony, and Swabia. The rise of feudalism created a complex patchwork of semi-independent principalities, all nominally under a German king but frequently pursuing their own interests. By the 10th century, the strongest German king, Otto I, defeated the Hungarians and was crowned Holy Roman Emperor, positioning Germany as the empire's heartland.

items.label: The Holy Roman Empire
items.summary: Germany became the core of a vast, decentralized empire spanning hundreds of semi-independent states and territories ruled by princes and bishops.
items.text: The Salian and Hohenstaufen dynasties consolidated German power within the Holy Roman Empire framework, but the empire never became centralized or truly unified. Hundreds of principalities, free cities, and ecclesiastical territories governed themselves with varying degrees of autonomy, creating a bewildering patchwork where local lords held more power than distant emperors. This fragmentation was partly formalized in 1356 by the Golden Bull of Charles IV, which established the system of seven Prince-Electors who chose the emperor from among German princes. German cities grew rich through trade in the Hanseatic League and the Rhine, while the printing press and Renaissance ideas spread from southern German cities to the rest of Europe.

items.label: The Reformation
items.summary: Martin Luther's challenge to Rome from Wittenberg sparked religious revolution that split Germany and Europe between Catholic and Protestant powers.
items.text: In 1517, the monk Martin Luther nailed his Ninety-Five Theses to a church door in Wittenberg, igniting a religious and political explosion that would tear the German territories apart. German princes embraced Lutheranism both from genuine faith and as a means to assert independence from Rome and the emperor, dividing the empire into hostile religious camps. The Thirty Years' War (1618–1648), fought largely on German soil, devastated the country with famine, disease, and military destruction, killing nearly a third of the German population. The Peace of Westphalia ended the war by recognizing the sovereignty of hundreds of German states, permanently fragmenting Germany into a collection of kingdoms, principalities, and city-states that would struggle to compete with unified nations like France and England.

items.label: The Age of Enlightened Absolutism
items.summary: German rulers embraced Enlightenment ideas while competing for power, with Prussia emerging as a rival to Austrian Habsburg dominance.
items.text: While France became Europe's dominant power under Louis XIV, German territories remained fragmented but increasingly ambitious. Frederick the Great of Prussia (r. 1740–1786) transformed his kingdom into a military and cultural powerhouse, defeating Austria in wars over Silesia and establishing Prussia as the second-greatest German power after Austria. German universities and courts became centers of philosophy, music, and science, producing Kant, Goethe, and Beethoven, even as political unity remained elusive. The rivalry between Prussia and Austria's Habsburg Empire would dominate German and European politics for the next century, with other German states caught between these two powers.

items.label: Napoleonic Wars and National Awakening
items.summary: Napoleon's armies shattered the Holy Roman Empire and German states, awakening German nationalism and prompting Prussia's rise as a unified power.
items.text: The French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars devastated German territories, with Napoleon dissolving the thousand-year-old Holy Roman Empire in 1806 and reorganizing German lands into the Confederation of the Rhine. Though initially oppressive, French occupation paradoxically sparked German national consciousness and romantic nationalism, as intellectuals and poets celebrated Germanic language, culture, and history against foreign domination. Prussia emerged from the Napoleonic Wars as Germany's strongest power after defeating Napoleon in the Wars of Liberation (1813–1815), and Berlin became the center of German national aspiration. The Congress of Vienna tried to restore order by creating the German Confederation (1815–1866), a loose union of 39 German states with Austria and Prussia as the major powers, but it failed to satisfy growing demands for German unity.

items.label: Revolution and Unification Failures
items.summary: Liberal revolutions swept Germany demanding unity and democracy, but conservative monarchies crushed the movements and Prussian militarism triumphed instead.
items.text: The Revolutions of 1848 saw German liberals and nationalists demand constitutional government and a unified German state, with the Frankfurt Parliament drafting a constitution that would have created a liberal empire under a German emperor. However, the Prussian king rejected the crown offered by the parliament, and military force gradually suppressed the revolutionary movements across German territories by 1849. Otto von Bismarck, appointed Prussian Chancellor in 1862, pursued German unification through a ruthless policy of 'blood and iron'—military might and realpolitik rather than idealism. Through three wars (against Denmark, Austria, and France), Bismarck defeated Austria, eliminated it from German affairs, and built a Prussian-dominated German Empire that excluded Austria but achieved German unification in 1871.

items.label: The German Empire and Imperial Rise
items.summary: Unified Germany under Prussian leadership became a militaristic industrial power that competed aggressively with France and Britain, destabilizing Europe.
items.text: The German Empire proclaimed in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles combined Prussian militarism with rapid industrial development, making Germany the continent's economic powerhouse by 1900. Chancellor Bismarck skillfully maintained the balance of power through elaborate alliances, but after his dismissal in 1890, Kaiser Wilhelm II pursued a more aggressive foreign policy of naval expansion and imperial rivalry that alarmed Britain and France. German workers built vast new cities and factories while labor movements and socialist parties grew; German science, culture, and philosophy flourished, even as militaristic values dominated state ideology and the Junker aristocracy retained political power. This aggressive imperial competition and nationalist fervor helped trigger World War I in 1914, which ultimately shattered the German Empire after four years of devastating trench warfare.

items.label: The Weimar Republic and Nazi Rise
items.summary: Germany's revolutionary transition to democracy collapsed under economic crisis and nationalist rage, enabling Hitler and the Nazis to seize power through legal means.
items.text: The German Empire's defeat in World War I triggered revolution; the Kaiser fled and the Weimar Republic was proclaimed in 1919 as a parliamentary democracy, though it was born from military defeat and resentment. The harsh Treaty of Versailles imposed massive reparations, territorial losses, and humiliation that poisoned German politics and democracy from the start, while hyperinflation in 1923 devastated the middle class and destroyed faith in democratic institutions. The Nazi Party under Adolf Hitler exploited this rage and despair, combining ultra-nationalism, antisemitism, and promises to restore German greatness into a potent political force that grew from a fringe movement to the largest party by 1932. In January 1933, the aging President Hindenburg appointed Hitler Chancellor, and within months he used the Reichstag Fire and emergency decrees to establish totalitarian Nazi dictatorship, crushing the Weimar Republic and all opposition.

items.label: Nazi Dictatorship and World War II
items.summary: Hitler's Nazi regime pursued aggressive expansionism and genocide, plunging Europe into World War II and murdering six million Jews in the Holocaust.
items.text: Hitler built a totalitarian police state that absorbed all aspects of German society into the Nazi Party's control, while his aggressive foreign policy remilitarized the Rhineland, annexed Austria and Czechoslovakia, and invaded Poland in September 1939, triggering World War II. The Nazi regime pursued the 'Final Solution'—the systematic murder of six million Jews along with millions of other targeted groups (Roma, disabled people, Slavs, political prisoners, and homosexuals)—in a genocide of unprecedented industrial scale and horror. German armies initially conquered vast territories but were ultimately defeated by the Soviet Union, Britain, and the United States, with the war ending in May 1945 only after Germany lay in ruins and Hitler had committed suicide. The war killed roughly seven million German soldiers and civilians, destroyed German cities through bombing, and left Germany completely defeated, occupied, and discredited, with no German state existing for the first time since 1871.

items.label: Division, Cold War, and Reunification
items.summary: Germany was divided between Soviet and Western occupation zones, becoming two separate states locked in Cold War hostility until Communism's collapse enabled reunification.
items.text: At the end of World War II, the victorious Allied powers divided Germany into four occupation zones (Soviet, American, British, and French), intending temporary administration but ultimately creating two separate states by 1949. West Germany became a prosperous capitalist democracy integrated into NATO and Western Europe, while East Germany became a Soviet satellite dictatorship with a command economy and brutal secret police (the Stasi) that imprisoned and executed thousands of dissidents. The Berlin Wall, built in 1961, became the physical symbol of Germany's division and the Cold War itself, splitting the country until 1989 when popular pressure and Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev's refusal to intervene allowed the wall to fall peacefully. German reunification in October 1990 brought the two states back together under West Germany's democratic system, making Berlin the capital once again and returning Germany to the center of European politics as a unified nation.

items.label: Modern United Germany
items.summary: Unified Germany is Europe's largest economy and a leader in the European Union and NATO, though it continues processing its Nazi past and navigating contemporary challenges.
items.text: Since reunification, Germany has become the economic and political heart of Europe, with a robust manufacturing sector, world-leading exports, and a stable social market economy that balances capitalism with welfare protections. Germany integrated Eastern territories into a unified political system while managing the psychological and economic scars of division, making impressive investments in East German infrastructure and gradually rebuilding a cohesive national identity. As a central European power, Germany has anchored NATO, led the European Union, and been crucial to European stability, though it has also faced complex moral questions about confronting its Nazi history and the responsibility that comes with being Europe's largest economy. In recent decades, Germany has grappled with immigration, climate change, Russian aggression in Ukraine, and internal political polarization, but it remains a democratic, prosperous nation committed to European integration and liberal values, a far cry from the militaristic empires and totalitarian dictatorship that defined much of its modern history.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Germany — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Order coffee by type, not size
items.text: Germans order by what they want—Espresso, Cappuccino, Americano—rather than small/medium/large. A standard Kaffee is a small black coffee; ask for Verlängerter if you want it slightly longer. Sizes vary by café, so don't assume standardization.

items.label: Café culture means sitting, not rushing
items.text: Germans linger in cafés for hours with one coffee. Waiters won't rush you or bring the bill unless asked. This is a place to read, work, or chat—not grab-and-go like some countries.

items.label: Avoid chain cafés in city centers
items.text: Starbucks exists but locals prefer independent Cafés or traditional Konditorei (bakery-cafés). Look for Wiener Melange in Vienna-style cafés for a proper cappuccino equivalent.

items.label: Kaffee mit Kuchen (coffee with cake)
items.text: The classic 3 p.m. ritual—a small coffee paired with a slice of Schwarzwälder Kirschtorte, Käsekuchen (cheesecake), or Apfelstrudel. Most cafés have excellent house-made cakes.

items.label: Schnitzel (breaded cutlet)
items.text: Thin-pounded pork or veal, breaded and fried golden. Serve with Zitrone (lemon) or Preiselbeersauce (lingonberry sauce). Order Wiener Schnitzel for veal, Schweineschnitzel for pork.

items.label: Spätzle (egg noodles)
items.text: Soft, irregular handmade noodles from Swabia, often topped with Käse (cheese) to become Käsespätzle. A comfort-food staple, especially in the south and Baden-Württemberg.

items.label: Sauerbraten (pot roast)
items.text: Beef marinated in vinegar and spices for days, then slow-cooked until tender. Traditionally served with red cabbage and potato dumplings—a winter must-try.

items.label: Currywurst (curry sausage)
items.text: Sliced grilled Bratwurst smothered in tangy curry ketchup, typically eaten standing at a street stand with fries. Berlin claims ownership; grab it at any Imbiss (fast food stand).

items.label: Schweinshaxe (pork knuckle)
items.text: A massive roasted pork leg, crispy-skinned and fall-apart tender, served with sauerkraut and mustard. Most famous in Bavaria; order it only if truly hungry.

items.label: Flammkuchen (Alsatian tart)
items.text: Crispy thin crust topped with crème fraîche, onion, and bacon—not quite pizza, not quite bread. A specialty along the France-Germany border, best eaten fresh from a wood oven.

items.label: Tagesgerichte are the best value
items.text: Most restaurants display daily specials (often a main with soup and bread) at significantly lower prices than the regular menu. These are usually very fresh and what locals eat.

items.label: Watch for portion sizes on the menu
items.text: German restaurants often list portion size (e.g., 200g) next to dishes. Main courses can be enormous; don't be shy about ordering just a starter and salad if you're not very hungry.

items.label: Beilagen (sides) cost extra
items.text: A main dish often comes with just the protein; vegetables, potatoes, or bread are listed separately and charged as Beilage. Check the menu carefully to avoid surprise costs.

items.label: Vegetarian options exist but aren't extensive
items.text: Germany is improving, but traditional restaurants still have limited meat-free choices. Look for Käsespätzle, Pilzrisotto, or Kartoffelsuppe; bigger cities have dedicated vegetarian restaurants.

items.label: Suppe (soup) is often a starter, not a main
items.text: German soups are typically light and come before the main course. Eintopf, however, is a hearty one-pot meal served as a main (often the cheapest option on the menu).

items.label: Gasthaus/Gasthof (traditional inn)
items.text: Family-run restaurants serving hearty, honest German food at reasonable prices. These are where locals eat; menus rarely change, quality is consistent, and atmosphere is warm and unpretentious.

items.label: Biergarten (beer garden)
items.text: Outdoor communal seating, self-service or table service, serving beer, sausages, and simple food. Most famous in Bavaria, but found in every city; bring your own food or eat theirs—either is acceptable.

items.label: Imbiss (street food stand)
items.text: Quick, cheap, standing-room-only counter serving Currywurst, Döner, Falafel, and grilled sausages. Perfect for lunch or late-night bites; quality varies wildly but often excellent.

items.label: Mensa (university canteen)
items.text: Open to non-students at lunch, offering subsidized meals (5–8€) of decent quality. Not fancy, but you'll eat alongside students at rock-bottom prices.

items.label: Weinstube (wine bar)
items.text: Cozy, often family-owned spots focused on wine (especially in wine regions like Mosel or Rheingau) with simple regional food. Expect excellent pairings and knowledgeable staff.

items.label: Avoid tourist-trap restaurants near major sights
items.text: Restaurants with picture menus and multilingual staff near train stations, cathedral squares, and monuments charge 2–3× normal prices for mediocre food. Walk 5–10 minutes away and quality and value improve dramatically.

items.label: Order beer by region and style
items.text: Pilsner dominates in the north, Weizen (wheat beer) in Bavaria, Dunkelweizen in darker forms. In some regions, ask for a Helles (pale lager) or ask the bartender what's local—never assume nationwide consistency.

items.label: Pint glasses are rare; beer comes in specific sizes
items.text: Standard is 0.5L (called Maß in Bavaria), but also 0.3L, 0.4L, or even 0.2L. Just say the size; don't ask for 'a beer' and expect to know what you'll get.

items.label: Weißbier (Bavarian wheat beer)
items.text: Cloudy, fruity, slightly sweet wheat beer, traditionally served in tall 0.5L glasses. Quintessential Bavarian summer drink; try brands like Schneider or Erdinger.

items.label: German wine is underrated and excellent
items.text: Rieslings from the Mosel, Rhine, and Rheingau regions are world-class and affordable. German labels look confusing (Kabinett, Spätlese, Auslese indicate sweetness/ripeness), but staff can explain; don't fear them.

items.label: Aperol Spritz is popular but not traditional
items.text: Germans enjoy local sparkling wines (Sekt), but Aperol Spritz has become trendy in cities. For authenticity, order Sekt, Glühwein (mulled wine in winter), or stick to beer.

items.label: Drinking culture is social, not about getting drunk
items.text: Germans nurse beers for hours and often don't drink heavily. Binge drinking exists but isn't the norm; expect measured consumption and conversation.

items.label: The waiter won't bring the bill until you ask
items.text: Saying 'Die Rechnung, bitte' ('The bill, please') is essential. Germans find it rude if staff rush you; taking your time is normal and expected, even in busy restaurants.

items.label: Tipping is expected but modest
items.text: Round up to the nearest euro or leave 5–10% for good service. Say 'Stimmt so' (keep the change) when paying if you're rounding up; Germans don't typically use tip jars.

items.label: Elbows on the table during eating are fine; keep hands visible
items.text: Unlike some cultures, resting elbows while eating is acceptable in Germany. However, hands should be visible on the table, never in your lap (that's considered suspicious).

items.label: Compliment the cook if eating well
items.text: If food is exceptional, saying 'Das schmeckt mir sehr gut' (it tastes very good to me) or 'Kompliment an den Koch' (compliments to the cook) is warmly received.

items.label: Splitting bills is common and straightforward
items.text: Germans don't shy away from going Dutch (Spaltung, 'splitting'). Just ask the waiter; they're used to it and will calculate per person without awkwardness.

items.label: Reservations are wise, especially outside tourist areas
items.text: Good local restaurants fill up, and walk-ins may wait 30+ minutes. Call ahead or use online booking (TheFork is popular); it costs nothing and guarantees a table.

items.label: Bavarian: Leberkäse and Obatzda
items.text: Leberkäse is a seasoned meatloaf served warm with mustard and bread; Obatzda is a pungent cheese spread with paprika and onions. Both are Munich classics, best at traditional beer halls.

items.label: Swabian: Maultaschen (German ravioli)
items.text: Stuffed pasta parcels filled with meat, spinach, and breadcrumbs, often served in broth or with lentil soup. A hearty, delicious specialty from Baden-Württemberg.

items.label: Rhineland: Sauerbraten and Himmel und Erde
items.text: Himmel und Erde (Heaven and Earth) pairs mashed potatoes with stewed apples and onions, often topped with fried blood sausage. A sweet-savory comfort food unique to the region.

items.label: Berlin: Eisbein and Pigeon Pea Soup
items.text: Eisbein is a boiled pork knuckle served with sauerkraut and mustard—simple, heavy, and very Berlin. Pair with Erbsensuppe (pea soup) for an authentic working-class meal.

items.label: Hamburg: Labskaus (sailor's stew)
items.text: A rough hash of corned beef, potatoes, and herring, topped with a fried egg and served with pickled herring on the side. Looks odd, tastes hearty; order it for the story.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Germany.

items.label: DB is the main operator
items.text: Deutsche Bahn (DB) runs most long-distance and regional trains across Germany. Book tickets online via db.de or the DB Navigator app for the best prices, especially for advance bookings and special offers like BahnCard discounts.

items.label: Regional alternatives exist
items.text: Operators like FlixTrain, Arriva, and regional companies offer cheaper alternatives on popular routes. Check Omio or GoEuro apps to compare all options before booking.
Strike action (Streiks) by DB staff occurs periodically and can cause major disruptions; always check current travel advisories and allow extra time during potential strike windows.

items.label: Validation not required
items.text: Unlike some European countries, German train tickets don't need stamping—just board with your valid ticket or mobile ticket displayed.
Travelling without a valid ticket incurs a €60 fine from ticket inspectors (Kontrolleure); they check frequently on busy routes.

items.label: U-Bahn and S-Bahn networks
items.text: U-Bahn (U) is the underground metro, while S-Bahn (S) is the suburban rail system. Both operate in major cities like Berlin, Munich, Hamburg, and Frankfurt; journey times are fast and frequent.

items.label: Validate your ticket at the machine
items.text: All local transit tickets must be validated (Entwertet) at yellow machines on platforms or inside vehicles before travel—unmarked tickets result in €60 fines.

items.label: Multi-day city passes save money
items.text: Most cities offer 1, 3, or 7-day passes that cover unlimited travel on buses, trams, U-Bahn, and S-Bahn; these are cheaper than buying individual tickets and often include tourist attractions.
Ticket inspectors (Kontrolleure) work undercover in civilian clothes and check regularly; they have legal authority and fines are enforced immediately or via debt collection.

items.label: Official taxis are reliable
items.text: Hail white or cream-colored taxis from taxi ranks (Taxistand) at stations and streets, or book via Taxi apps like TaxiDE. Meters start at €2.50–€3.50 and charge per kilometer and waiting time.

items.label: Uber and Bolt are available
items.text: Both Uber and Bolt operate in major German cities; prices are often lower than traditional taxis but surge during peak hours. Always check the quoted fare before confirming.
Unlicensed 'taxi' offers at airports and train stations are common scams—always use official ranks or apps to avoid overcharging and safety risks.

items.label: Card payments standard
items.text: Most taxis and ride apps accept card payments, though some older taxis still prefer cash; confirm payment method when booking or getting in.

items.label: License and documents required
items.text: You must carry a valid passport/ID, driving license, vehicle registration, insurance documents, and an International Driving Permit (recommended for non-EU licenses). A green insurance card is advisable.
Speed limits are strictly enforced: 50 km/h in towns, 100 km/h outside towns, 120 km/h on motorways (Autobahn). Cameras catch speeding and fines start at €15–€70+ depending on excess.

items.label: Motorway vignette (Plakette) required
items.text: Vehicles need a windshield sticker (€10 for 10 days, €30 for one year) to use motorways; buy them at petrol stations, post offices, or online before driving.
Drink-driving limit is 0.05% BAC; driving over 0.08% is a criminal offense with license suspension, fines of €500–€1500, and possible jail time.

items.label: Environmental zones in cities
items.text: Major cities like Berlin, Munich, and Cologne have low-emission zones (Umweltzone) requiring a colored sticker based on vehicle age; rentals usually come with this included.

items.label: Parking meters and regulations
items.text: Paid parking (Parkplatz) is common in city centers; use parking meters, mobile apps like ParkNow, or pay-and-display tickets. Parking fines are €15–€100+.

items.label: Major hubs and budget airlines
items.text: Frankfurt, Munich, Berlin, and Düsseldorf are the largest hubs; budget carriers like Ryanair, Wizz Air, and EasyJet operate from secondary airports. Check which airport your booking uses.

items.label: Train connections are fast
items.text: Most German airports have direct S-Bahn or express train connections to city centers (e.g., Frankfurt to downtown in 12 minutes, Munich in 45 minutes); this is faster and cheaper than taxis.
Airport staff strikes (Streiks) occasionally occur and cause flight delays or cancellations; check airline updates before travel, especially during labor negotiations.

items.label: Arrive early for security
items.text: Allow 2–3 hours before international flights; security queues can be long, especially at Frankfurt and Munich during peak hours.

items.label: River cruises and local ferries
items.text: Cities like Berlin (Spree), Hamburg (Elbe), and Cologne (Rhine) offer scenic ferry and boat services; these count as local transit on city passes or require separate tickets (€5–€20 per journey).

items.label: Island and lake ferries
items.text: German lakes (like Lake Constance/Bodensee) and islands in the North Sea and Baltic have regular ferry services; book online or buy tickets at ticket offices on arrival.

items.label: Included in city transit passes
items.text: Many city day passes include local ferry services; check details when buying your pass to avoid duplicate ticket purchases.

items.label: Single tickets vs. day passes
items.text: Buy single tickets for one or two journeys; day/multi-day passes (€10–€50) are worth it for frequent travel within cities or regions and often cover companions.

items.label: BahnCard discounts on national trains
items.text: BahnCard 25 (€62 per year) or BahnCard 50 (€285 per year) give 25% or 50% discounts on DB tickets; the 25 version often pays for itself after 2–3 trips.

items.label: Deutsche Bahn app for mobile tickets
items.text: Download DB Navigator to book, pay, and store digital train tickets on your phone; no paper needed and easier than queuing at ticket counters.
Counterfeit or altered tickets are common; buy only from official sources (DB.de, station counters, authorized shops) to avoid fines and confiscation.

items.label: Group discounts available
items.text: Groups of 6+ traveling together on DB trains get automatic discounts; book together or at the same time to activate group pricing.

items.label: Prepaid SIM cards are easy to buy
items.text: Purchase SIM cards at supermarkets (Rewe, Edeka), mobile shops (Vodafone, Telekom, O2), or airports; prices start at €10–€20 with included credit. Activation is instant.

items.label: Major carriers: Telekom, Vodafone, O2
items.text: These three have the best coverage nationwide; Telekom is most reliable in rural areas. Prepaid plans include data, calls, and SMS for €20–€40 per month or €10–€15 per week.

items.label: eSIM option for new phones
items.text: Telekom, Vodafone, and O2 offer eSIM plans; download an eSIM app and activate digitally without a physical card—faster for travelers.

items.label: Free WiFi is widespread
items.text: Free WiFi is available at most train stations, airports, cafes, and hotels; download the DB Navigator app to access WiFi on long-distance trains.
EU roaming is cheap but check caps; some providers throttle or charge after heavy usage despite 'unlimited' plans—use local SIM or WiFi for large data needs.
